Recovery Services vaults are heavily concentrated in eastus with RS0 as the dominant SKU and infrastructure encryption enabled: because 68.9% of vaults are in eastus and 97.8% use RS0 with infra-encryption, you should provision matching RS0 Recovery Services vaults in the west region with the same encryption settings and include them as ASR targets in the failover runbook.

TrafficManager is used tenant-wide as a global DNS traffic control mechanism: given 294 Traffic Manager profiles shown as global, prefer implementing DNS-based failover/failback using Traffic Manager (or Azure Front Door where already used) with health probes and automated profile updates rather than manual IP reassignments.

A large share of storage accounts are Standard_LRS with a smaller but meaningful portion using GRS/RAGRS: because ~48% are Standard_LRS and ~23% are Standard_GRS/17% RAGRS, treat application data as not universally geo-redundant and include explicit cross-region replication or application-level replication (or ensure ASR replication for VM disks) for critical data in your DR plan.

Virtual networks and NSGs are regionally concentrated with consistent subnet naming (e.g., default, appSubnet): since eastus is the largest VNet location and common subnet names appear across VNets, mirror VNet and NSG configurations in the west region with the same subnet names to enable repeatable automation and straightforward ASR network mapping.

Virtual machines overwhelmingly use managed identities (system and user-assigned): because ~98.8% of VMs include SystemAssigned or SystemAssigned+UserAssigned identities, include steps to ensure required user-assigned identities exist (or are recreated) in west and update role assignments during failover/failback automation.

Public IPs predominantly use the Standard SKU and are regional: with ~96% Standard public IPs and counts in both eastus and westus2, avoid relying on moving existing public IPs between regions and instead implement DNS failover plus provision new Standard regional IPs in the secondary region where needed.

Key Vaults enforce soft-delete and purge-protection with long retention: given soft-delete retention is typically 90 days and enablepurgeprotection is True, include Key Vault backup/restore or cross-region secret replication in your DR plan and account for purge-protection when designing automated failback procedures.

Many disks are Premium_LRS indicating performance-sensitive VMs: because ~63% of disks use Premium_LRS, ensure the west region has equivalent managed disk SKUs and quota available and include disk SKU parity and resize/quota validation as part of the replication/runbook steps.

Existing Recovery Services vaults often use user-assigned identities (50%): since half the vaults have UserAssigned identities, match the identity model when creating secondary vaults and script the necessary identity provisioning and RBAC assignments into your DR automation to avoid permission gaps during failover.
